displayTitle: 2025-08-05 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Overseas Investment (National Interest Test and Other Matters) Amendment Bill [Subcommittee A, Part 2]
topic: Overseas Investment (National Interest Test and Other Matters) Amendment Bill
description: Tuesday 5 August 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Overseas Investment (National Interest Test and Other Matters) Amendment Bill Dame Anne Salmond Dr Geoff Bertram Colin Robertson Jodi ELLIS
descriptionSummary: Overseas Investment (National Interest Test and Other Matters) Amendment Bill. Dame Anne Salmond. Dr Geoff Bertram

chunks.text: Particularly those that further loosen and centralize decision making about overseas investments of the kind that have already proven to be economically, socially, and environmentally disastrous for New Zealand economically. Sound decisions about overseas investments in New Zealand require robust, rigorous, and impartial risk assessment.

In the case of industrial forestry, for example, on which I focused in my written submission, this requires an in-depth knowledge of local ge, the dynamics of local waterways, marine environments, and local communities. It's just not possible for ministers or the overseas investment office or indeed central government to have this kind of localized expertise.

Unfortunately, industrial forestry demonstrates a long history of ill-informed decisions by central government with devastating impacts on local environments, um, regional economies and local communities. So I'll speak about ity, which is the region which I know best with some of the most erodable landscapes in the world.

And these policies from central government over time have included initially the clear failing and burning of native forests, followed by attempts to control the resulting absolutely devastating erosion with plantations of shallow rooting, relatively short-lived and highly flammable. Exotic monocultures then privatizing these plantations in the eighties, selling them off eventually to overseas companies, and allowing them to be clear, felt followed by trying to control the resulting devastating impacts after cyclone Ebola, for example,

by repeating the same cycle with foresters incentivized to plant plantations of, uh, conifers across steep, highly erodible landscapes right down to the banks of streams and rivers, and then allowing them to be felt again and replanted. These impacts on the region have include radical erosion, some of the worst in the world, and losses of top soil rivers across the re region that are even more sediment laden than the Yang Sea in China.

Harbors and offshore fisheries filled with sediment destroying shell fisheries, as well as fisheries. Denuded hillsides stripped back to bedrock in many catchments. Now up the headwaters and rising river beds that exponentially are increasing the risk of catastrophic flooding to coastal communities, including Gisborne city, which is built on a floodplain and widespread destruction of roads, bridges, fences, paddocks, orchards, farm buildings, family homes, public facilities, the loss of life, the disruption of communications and water supplies

and devastating impacts on every other region. Every other industry in the region costing billions of dollars from Ill thought through overseas investments in forestry. Most recently, there's the ETS incentive to leave untended pine plantations as carbon sinks full of weeds and pests as an extreme fire risk for all other landowners that yields little or no employment only profit, uh, profits for overseas investors since about the 1920, central governments have repeated the same mistakes over and over again, or invented new ones like the ETS,

the proposed amendments to the OIA, um, relating to forestry follow exactly the same pattern, ensuring that the cycle of stupidity will keep on happening until there's nothing left to wreck the harm caused by some international forestry companies. I've outlined this in my written submission and the exorbitant ongoing costs they've inflicted on taxpayers and local communities with poor forestry practices.

Some of the worst in the world are documented in judgments by courts, by the auditors of the Forestry Stewardship Council in Bon, who have just absolutely slammed what's going on in forestry in tti, and in the graphic report of an ministerial inquiry after cyclone Gabrielle, after these experiences and the recent floods in Tasman, I find it absolutely inexcusable, unforgivable that this government is proposing to loosen rather than tighten controls on overseas forestry investments in New Zealand.

And I would implore you rather than removing or making, um, not making mandatory the benefit to New Zealand test, this must remain mandatory for overseas investments in forestry, and it shouldn't include, it should be strengthened to include a, a benefit to region test that takes local geologies and economic impacts into account in relation to specific investments as they're proposed informed by advice, advice from the region.

The investor test must remain mandatory and it should be strengthened to include a rigorous examination of the previous record of an international forestry companies before they're allowed to operate in our country. And these tests ought to be carried out with very strong safeguards to avoid the possibility of ministerial capture by lobbyists and forestry in interests, and to ensure the credibility of the claimed benefits to New Zealand and the region.

And I was just recently in bond talking to the head, um, of, um, of ethics for the whole world at the Forestry Stewardship Council. And I can tell you that from that international vantage point, what is going on in forestry practices in our region in TDI are just absolutely deplored, um, globally as well as locally.

So please do not loosen these controls. Please strengthen them. Please make sure that when you make overseas investments on behalf of our country, you make them with some rigorous application of good knowledge and understanding of local conditions. Thank you very much.

Okay. Oh, thank you. We are at time, but, uh, we, we appreciate that you've joined us Dam. So I am, um, certainly, um, willing to give someone a question or, or someone to make, uh, or seek any clarity. Not that clarity is probably required after, um, your presentation, um, Damien, but let's just see if we've got any hands up.

And no, I think you've made yourself, um, uh, explicitly clear, um, both in your written submission and your oral. So thank you for, for, um, uh, your patience this afternoon. And thanks for appearing before our committee. Well, I'm speaking for, I'm on the transitional advisory group in Gisborne, and I'm speaking for everybody in our region pretty much, except for a very few foresters.

Yes, yes. We can hear you loud and clear. Okay. Thanks very much. Um, yeah. When I was a young economist at the University of Oxford back in the seventies, I spent 10 years working on the economic history of Peru. Um, and one of the central topics that we addressed in that study was the role of foreign direct investment in the Peruvian economy over a hundred years from the 1890s to the 19, the late 1970s.

Um, and, uh, I learned two things from, from that period of, of study and research. One of them is that, uh, economics does give you very powerful tools for evaluating foreign direct investments. Um, but economics is not the only discipline that you need to turn to, to get a, a full picture of costs and benefits.

And so a comprehensive cost benefit assessment of the impact of a foreign direct investment is quite a, quite a solid job, and it needs to go beyond the boundaries of what mainstream economics, uh, can contribute, although that's very important. The second thing that I learned is that, uh, you can't prejudge the implications of a foreign investment until you've had a look at it.

Um, foreign investments do span a very wide range from some that are constructive and, and, and produce benefits for the host economy to others that are simply damaging predatory and corrupting. And, uh, in the Peruvian case, we encountered more of the second than of the first, I have to say.

Um, but you have to embark on the study of any particular project, I think with a willingness to look seriously and rigorously at all the issues that it, that it raises. And so I'd refer you guys to the Nobel Prize in economics this year, which went to, um, Aaron slu and, uh, some colleagues of his, uh, for, for their book, which is called Why Nations Fail.

Um, and the essential point that they make in that book is that the institutional setup, uh, is absolutely vital to, in determining whether a nation is prone to rent seeking corruption, predation, exclusionary, uh, conduct, um, or inclusive, uh, constructive, participatory and all the rest of it.

And obviously you can guess which way they, they judge the thing. Um, now my point in relation to this bill is that, uh, it seems to me to be pointing New Zealand very firmly, um, against all the lessons of my nation's fail, um, towards, uh, an uncritical and predetermined approach to evaluating foreign direct investment projects.

Um, basically a blocking off of the opportunity to truly explore the detriments as well as the benefits of any particular project. Um, a strong degree of predetermination, uh, in the sense that all projects are expected to be good, when in fact history tells us that plenty are bad and, and should not proceed.

Um, and, uh, it, it worries me very, very greatly that, uh, rather than moving towards more rigorous assessment and more thorough appro, um, investigation of costs and benefits, this bill is contributing to moving away from that in the direction that the, uh, that the existing overseas investment Act already does.

And in my written submission, I've outlined, um, one or two of the things in the existing act, uh, that I think predetermine, um, the outcomes of the overseas investment investigations in a direction that opens the way for us to recruit the worst rather than the best of overseas investors.

Um, there's a, the saying in economics, bad money drives out good. Um, leaving the playing field wide open does provide an op an invitation to the more predatory and extractive side of the international investment community, and can be discouraging, in fact, to the constructive and useful side.

So I really do think that the whole question of what is in the national interest and how it is looked after by the evaluation process, um, remains pretty unclear in this bill. But, uh, as I read it between the lines, the whole thrust is towards an uncritical predetermined approval process, uh, which is actually open to lobbying and corruption at, at several stages along the way, and which ultimately comes to rest very much on the, uh, individual determinations made by the regulator and the minister, um, without any obvious need for them

to expose very clearly to the public the basis in which they've reached those conclusions. So I do urge you to recommend that this bill not proceed. Um, happy to take any questions. Okay. Thank you very much. Uh, Dr. Bertram, um, happy for questions. We've got Dr. Deborah Russell.
Oh, Deborah, you're just on mute.

It's probably quite lucky. I was on mute for a moment there. I said, thank you. Um, thank you for your, um, submission. Um, it's really, really interesting. Um, I'm, I'm just, I suppose I wanna just draw attention to, I mean, if I'm reading it at a very broad level, it, it's talking about how that the test we've got on this bill don't take into a account water broader sort of wellbeing, um, issues and broader, um, externalities to a, an investment decision.

Um, so I'm, I'm just sort of stacking it up now with the, the public finance amendment bill, where we are removing the wellbeing tests. Do you have any comments on that? Uh, well, to be perfectly honest, I find the wellbeing test, uh, at the moment not to be really tightly enough specified to give a fully operational frame of reference for making policy.

Um, I, I, I've been a little disappointed in the attempts to, um, to actually frame a wellbeing, um, analytical process that, that, that can actually weed things out or, or, or guide decision making. Um, there's an awful lot of, of nice words, um, and, and things that feel good, but I, I guess I'm committed to a much, um, a much more brutal, I suppose, and cold frame of reference for making, making decisions.

But I do think that, you know, there are really serious externalities covered by that wellbeing framework. Um, and they are to do with the sort of pressures that foreign investments, particularly in areas like mining, um, can put onto, uh, an economy where natural resources are not unlimited and where environmental concerns are really important.

Um, but just a, but just another example of an externality. It's one that's been worrying me quite a lot. Um, you'll be familiar with the fact that Kalka, now, Rio Tinto runs a smelter down south. And, um, over, over the last 30 years, I've sort of been keeping an eye on that because it's exactly one of the sort of projects where the economics is quite interesting to follow.

Um, and one of the big spread effects from the Rio Tinto smelter, uh, which you would've observed over the last 10 years, is that it's a major factor in determining the price that electricity comes to ordinary households at keeping, uh, that smelter by merely existing, keeps hunt going, uh, and keeps electricity prices high.

And you would've seen that when the smelter looked like closing down a few years ago. Um, all the, uh, gen Taylor's share prices dropped immediately for the very good reason that, uh, the smelter is fundamental to the profitability of a completely different industry, uh, and the wellbeing of a wide range of electricity consumers across the country.

Uh, I don't now, now for me, that's what the National Interest test, uh, needs to be thinking about things as wide as that. Uh, that seems to me that the framework within which this bill sets is much more likely to, to narrow things right down away from that. Uh, but if, if, for example, you're looking forward to having data centers coming here, if you've been watching what the, uh, what Google, Amazon Meta and the others have been doing in the construction of, of data centers for, for developing their AI large language models over in the

States, they are now putting enormous pressure on the electricity system in the United States. Um, and I anticipate that if under this bill we fast track a a couple of seriously large, um, internet data centers, uh, you'll, you'll have another, another 10 years of ordinary electricity consumers carrying high prices while large international corporates, uh, sign up deals for cut price power on the Rio Tinto model.

And that worries me because it's very difficult in advance to get a grasp of the consequences of the sort of these sort of deals, which will be done under commercial confidentiality until they're announced, and then they'll be, they'll burst upon you. Um, this is just a little, little red flag warning for you. Thank you. That's most instructive.
